data.title: Hard To Imagine A Retirement Portfolio Without These 3 Picks
data.description:
I think that most of you would agree with me that retirement investing is primarily about defense, durability and ensuring that the risk of outliving the saved asset base is really taken out of the equation.

The other side of retirement investing (once the risks are managed) is to enhance the return side. And this is where things start to become tricky as investors have to strike a balance between acceptable risk and acceptable return.

For example, if one invested in only bank deposits, investment-grade bonds, and, say, gold, the returns would be minimal. Theoretically, it could be fine to do so provided that the accumulated capital is very large so that the return is almost irrelevant as long as the wealth is preserved on an inflation-adjusted basis.

This is not my case.

In my case, I am still in my young thirties, trying to compound the necessary amount of passive income snowball by using as minimal capital as possible, while keeping the risk-check-boxes filled.

There are three broad risk parameters that I try to avoid:

  1. Dividend cut.
  2. Loss of dividend purchasing power.
  3. Permanent impairment of capital.

To tick these boxes, it usually boils down to investment-grade businesses with conservative dividend coverage levels that are supported by defensive and stable cash-flowing business models.

I will present now 2 picks that form the core of my retirement income portfolio.

#1 - Brookfield Infrastructure Partners

Brookfield Infrastructure Partners L.P. (BIP)(BIP.UN:CA) is an infrastructure play, which is automatically introduces several attractive characteristics to the strategy that I outlined above in the intro section.

The infrastructure as such is a defensive asset, which helps preserve the purchasing power of the contracted cash flows. Most of the contracts between the private developer (like BIP) and the public authorities include CPI-clauses or fixed revenue bumps.

In the figure below, we can nicely see how the overall business model works.

There is a CPI component, a demand or use-based component, and, importantly, the reinvested growth component. Together, these three pieces should secure a long-term growth of around 7%.

If we look deeper into the BIP's fundamentals, we will see that there is a lot of firepower to accommodate the growth sustainably.

The FFO payout is ~67%, which means that a notable amount of cash is left inside the business to fund new growth projects.

The balance sheet is at an investment-grade level with a well-laddered debt maturity profile, which helps keep both the interest rate and refinancing risk low.

Plus, one of BIP's tactical funding sources is the asset rotation program, where it sells full and/or partial stakes of completed projects to crystallize value and access fresh liquidity.

All of these elements are clearly supportive of capturing incremental growth (on top of the organic levers). For instance, this will come in handy to cover the data center play, which in 2024 resulted in an FFO growth of 21%.

Finally, we have to also appreciate the fact that BIP has managed to increase its dividend for 16 consecutive years with the most recent increase at 6%.

BIP at a current yield of 5.1% looks very attractive.

#2 - Main Street Capital

Main Street Capital Corporation (MAIN) is another passive income machine, that has delivered consistent dividends throughout the periods such as the GFC, COVID-19, and interest rate surge shock.

For some MAIN might seem a counterintuitive retirement pick because of its business model that is associated with private credit lending. Private credit tends to involve high risks as the lending is made towards relatively small businesses that cannot easily access capital from traditional banks.

However, MAIN is different.

The reason it has been able to distribute stable and gradually growing dividends is because of its prudent capital allocation approach.

Here are the main points.

First, MAIN has not only a traditional BDC investment portfolio, but also an external asset management arm, which offers a truly passive income source that has a way lower risk profile than general BDC lending.

Second, MAIN lends/invests in high-quality businesses (on a first-lien basis) that have already well-established cash flow profiles. For instance, it has one of the strongest portfolio credit statistics in the industry. The weighted average senior leverage is at 2.8x EBITDA, and senior interest coverage to EBITDA is at 2.4x.

Third, MAIN holds on average 39% of equity ownership in all LMM portfolio companies, where more than 65% of these equity investments produce already consistent dividends. In practice, it creates an opportunity for MAIN to access equity-like returns from investment monetization.

Finally, MAIN has one of the lowest debt levels in the sector. The debt-to-equity stands at 0.81x, which is a level that can easily support the portfolio growth in a prudent manner without increasing the overall financial risk.

At a current dividend yield of 6.8% and the aforementioned fundamental, MAIN is a clear long-term income buy.

#3 - Realty Income

Realty Income Corporation (O) is my favorite REIT, which has one of the strongest fundamentals in the sector. It has also had one of the weakest total return results since the moment when the Fed started to hike the interest rates. While BIP and MAIN have both seen their share prices go up over the past several quarters, O still remains in a relatively depressed territory, which makes it an interesting tactical opportunity as well.

When it comes to defense, it is difficult to find a more protected REIT. For example, it is one of the few REITs that carry an upper investment grade balance sheet, which in combination with a market cap of ~$48 billion allows O to keep the WACC as low as possible. In the REIT business, which is primarily about capturing spreads between cap rates and WACC, having a low WACC is strategically (from the long-term value perspective) important.

An argument that I often hear from the Realty Income bears is that O has a weak portfolio that is subject to a very minimal growth rate.

Here is the thing.

First, it is not only about cap rates and the income side, but also about the cost of capital, where O has a clear advantage.

Second, I think many investors have not yet appreciated this, but O is slowly but surely diversifying away from the traditional net-lease retail model. About 20% of the NOI now stems from the high multiple (growth) property segment.

Third, as of Q3, 2024, O had a weighted average lease maturity of just above 10 years, which nicely mitigates the vacancy risk, and, instead, offers a secured organic growth trajectory on top of the incremental FFO that comes from new growth projects.

Finally, speaking of new growth projects or property acquisitions, we have to definitely factor in the fact that ~25% of the annual FFO generation is retained in O's books. This is a power source for funding additional growth without being fully dependent on the balance sheet, which is, obviously, a clear advantage in terms of risk mitigation and sustainable growth.

At a dividend yield of 5.7%, an upper investment grade balance sheet, and a dividend aristocrat status, it would be hard to imagine not having O in my retirement income portfolio.

The bottom line

In my opinion, retirement investing is about keeping the risks low first, and then only thinking about how to enhance the income profile. Having said that, this does not necessarily mean that we have to assume only inflation-level returns. In fact, by being selective and putting the work in (due diligence, fundamental analysis), we can find many picks that offer attractive risk-to-reward positions.

In this article, I have presented three picks, which produce durable and enticing dividends - all of which could be easily included in the retirement income portfolios without assuming unacceptable risks.

data.title: Wolters Kluwer: Very Well Managed, But Too Expensive For Me
data.description:
Introduction

Wolters Kluwer N.V. (OTCPK:WOLTF, OTCPK:WTKWY) is one of the world’s largest companies providing information, services, and software solutions to professionals. Wolters Kluwer is predominantly focusing on tax & accounting, financial and corporate compliance and the healthcare segments. The customer base is very sticky, and this provides Wolters Kluwer with excellent visibility. Unfortunately, there aren’t a lot of non-organic growth opportunities, and that’s one of the main reasons why the company spends the majority of its free cash flow on share buybacks.

Wolters Kluwer has its primary listing on Euronext Amsterdam, where it is trading with WKL as its ticker symbol. The average daily volume in Amsterdam is approximately 440,000 shares, so I’d strongly recommend to trade in Wolters stock where the trading volumes are the highest. The current market cap is approximately 41.7B EUR, based on an anticipated share count of just under 235M shares and the current share price of 177.50 EUR. I will use the Euro as base currency throughout this article.

Robust cash flows — but that doesn't make the stock cheap

Wolters Kluwer does not provide detailed financial results on a quarterly basis, and to figure out the company’s earnings profile for FY 2024 (and the next few years), I have to fall back on the H1 2024 financial statements. Those are the most recent detailed financial statements, and those were followed up on with a trading update and guidance reconfirmation.

So let’s use the H1 2024 financial statements as the starting point. In the first semester of last year, the company generated a total revenue of 2.89B EUR, which represented a 6.1% increase on a reported basis. The COGS increased as well but at a much slower pace of just around 3%. This boosted the gross profit from 1.95B EUR to 2.09B EUR, a 7.4% increase.

As the income statement above shows, Wolters Kluwer had a good grip on its operating expenses. The sales costs and G&A expenses increased, but those increases weren’t outrageous, and this helped to post a pre-tax profit of 665M EUR and a net income of 509M EUR which represents an EPS of 2.13 EUR.

Looking at the cash flow statement, Wolters Kluwer reported an operating cash flow of 622M EUR. However, this included a 117M EUR investment in its working capital position while it also included a 171M EUR cash tax payment although only 156M EUR was due, as per the income statement. Additionally, we should deduct the 31M EUR in lease payments from the equation. This results in an adjusted operating cash flow of 723M EUR.

Wolters’ capex requirements are quite low and in the first half of 2024, the company spent just 147M EUR on its capital expenditures. This means the underlying free cash flow was approximately 576M EUR. Divided over the 237.4M shares that were issued and outstanding in June, the net free cash flow represents 2.43 EUR per share. A decent result and the operating margins came in as expected. And as Wolters Kluwer continued to buy back its shares (the net share count decreased by 0.5% in the third quarter), the per-share result should continue to outpace the net profit in absolute numbers. After the end of the third quarter, Wolters Kluwer confirmed the final tranche of the buyback plan was on track to be spent by the end of 2024. This means I expect the net share count to drop below 235M shares. Additionally, Wolters Kluwer has approved a 100M EUR share buyback program to be completed in the first two months of 2025.

This was followed up on with a trading update later in the year. Wolters Kluwer confirmed its revenue growth was still tracking at approximately 6% (both in constant currencies and organically). More interestingly, the recurring revenue growth was estimated at 7% while the non-recurring revenue increase was just 2%. A substantial portion of the recurring revenue growth was cloud-related, as the organic revenue growth in that segment was an impressive 16%.

The company also reconfirmed its full-year guidance. As you can see below, WKL expects a small margin expansion as the midpoint of the adjusted operating profit margin guidance is 20 bps higher than the 2023 margin. I was more interested in the cash flow guidance and was pleased to see Wolters Kluwer was guiding for an adjusted free cash flow result of 1.15-1.2B EUR (using an exchange rate of 1.08 USD per Euro). This would represent an increase of approximately 1% compared to the 2023 free cash flow result if I would use the midpoint of the 2024 guidance.

Assuming a net share count of 235M shares at the end of 2024, the net free cash flow per share would come in at approximately 5 EUR per share.

Investment thesis

While we can and should expect additional growth in 2025 and beyond, the growth will likely be limited to a low to mid-single digit revenue and free cash flow increase. Looking at the consensus estimates for 2025 and 2026, analysts are calling for an EBITDA of 2.1B EUR and 2.25B EUR respectively, resulting in a net profit of 1.18B EUR and 1.28B EUR in those two years. The net free cash flow should be approximately 5-10% higher.

This means Wolters Kluwer is currently trading at a forward free cash flow yield of approximately 3.2-3.3% based on the expectations for 2026 and assuming the net share count decreases to 227M shares by the end of 2026.

I like the low capital intensity of the business, and I like how Wolters Kluwer is run. But it appears to be a company one needs to buy during an economic crisis (but even during the COVID-19 pandemic, the stock didn’t get really cheap). While I like the company’s financial performance, the stock is too expensive for me and a “hold” rating appears reasonable.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Century Communities Offers Little Upside For Now (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
The latest earnings season has been brutal for homebuilders. Century Communities, Inc. (NYSE:CCS) provided a small respite after the stock actually gained 4.8% on the first day of trading after reporting results for Q4 2024. Still, that gain was just more noise in the middle of a period of price indecision for the stock. Eventually, the weight of sobering news from other builders and poor housing data pushed CCS toward its lows of the year. Builders like Tri Pointe Homes, Inc. (TPH) and Toll Brothers, Inc. (TOL) took big hits this week after disappointing investors. Moreover, builder sentiment plunged to a 5-month low. An extremely high 13-point drop in sentiment for 6-month sales expectations delivered a potentially ominous message for the spring selling season. An 8.4% sequential drop in housing starts, a 1.8% year-over-year decline, further reinforced a sobering outlook. Given this backdrop, tepid guidance from CCS for 10% order and delivery growth for 2025 and 2026 and declining margins suggest the stock will have minimal upside in the near term. Accordingly, I downgraded CCS to neutral.

Slowing Business Pace

CCS set a number of corporate records in Q4 2024 and for the full year. From the earnings conference call transcript:

  • Full-year record deliveries of 11,007 homes (up 15% year-over-year).
  • Community count of 322 (up 28% year-over-year).
  • Quarterly record deliveries of 3,198 homes (up 1.4% year-over-year).
  • Quarterly record home sales revenues of $1.2B (up 5% year-over-year).

These records are the result of managing the company for incremental growth quarter-by-quarter.

Yet, the guidance indicates a slowdown in business performance. Despite a 28% growth in communities and an expected future growth percentage in the mid to high single-digit range, CCS only expects a 9.5% growth in deliveries for 2025 (at the midpoint of an 11,700 to 12,400 range) and 2026, significantly down from 2024’s 15% growth. The high side of guidance holds out the prospect for 12.7% growth, and the low side cautions growth could be as low as 6.3%. Expected home sales revenue of $4.5B to $4.8B is just 5.7% higher at the midpoint than 2024’s full-year revenue of $4.4B.

This growth slowdown comes with expected absorption flat-lining at a rate of 3.2. The company is managing a delicate balance between affordability of its homes, sales pace, and margins. Incentives sit at the center of these tensions. While full-year margins increased slightly by 80 basis points to 23.3%, margin performance in Q4 dragged on results. For Q3, CCS recorded an adjusted gross margin of 23.6% which declined to 22.9% in Q4. The company anticipates further weakness in Q1 2025 even as SG&A falls as a percent of revenue. Incentives on closed homes in Q4 increased to 800 basis points from 700 basis points in Q3 and climbed to 900 basis points for Q4 orders. CCS did not provide full year guidance margins but did acknowledge that “for the first quarter 2025, we expect our homebuilding gross margin to ease on a sequential basis.”

A graph of TTM (trailing twelve month) revenue vs. EPS vs. gross profit shows in stark relief CCS’s struggle for profitable growth. While revenue is finally climbing back to the peak in 2022, both gross profit and EPS remain well off their peaks. Moreover, the recent uptrend in revenue has diverged from gross profit and a relatively flat EPS profile.

Note Century Communities is also trimming its footprint ever so slightly to meet affordability needs. The company reported a 2% year-over-year decrease in overall square footage for 2024. ASP (average selling price) still increased 4% for the year.

Valuation

Valuation is always the temptress for homebuilders. Valuation is typically low but can go even lower. CCS sports valuation metrics below both sector median and its own 5-year average. Most telling is a price/book ratio below 1.0 which means the stock is priced for recession. The 0.9 TTM and 0.8 forward price/book ratios are both well below CCS’s 5-year averages of 1.1 and 1.0 respectively. The sector medians are more than double these levels. While price/sales are around CCS’s 5-year average, they are roughly half the sector median. The price/earnings rations (GAAP) are the same story. Thus, while CCS has a tempting low valuation, it is not trading at a sufficient discount to itself to warrant a buy hoping for an upside surprise later in the year.

Conclusion

The stakes are high for CCS in 2025 after significantly underperforming the iShares U.S. Home Construction ETF in 2024 a 19.5% loss on the year versus ITB’s 1.8% gain. Since the end of the trough year 2022, CCS is up 44.5% versus ITB’s 65.8%. Year-to-date, CCS has avoided new lows unlike ITB which is down 2.8% year-to-date. CCS is down 1.5%. If it can hold the lows for support, it has a decent chance to to out-perform ITB for the rest of the year. Even for the seasonal trade on homebuilders, the technicals do not favor a speculative buy on CCS until at least a breakout above the down trending 50-day moving average (DMA) (the red line below). This trendline has capped CCS since the October earnings report.

So while CCS looks relatively cheap to its peers, it is not cheap enough relative to itself to warrant the risk for what looks like minimal upside in the near-term. The slowing growth profile and declining margins (mainly due to affordability efforts) augur poorly for the critical spring selling season and puts growth guidance for 2026 at risk.

Be careful out there!

data.title: Allspring Disciplined U.S. Core Fund Q4 2024 Commentary
data.description:
Market review

The S&P 500 Index (SP500, SPX) gained 2.41% during the fourth quarter of 2024. Led once again by mega-cap, technology-focused companies, U.S. equities moved higher over the removal of the U.S. election uncertainty, solid economic growth, lower rates, healthy corporate earnings, and expectations of a more business-friendly regulatory environment. West Texas Intermediate crude oil prices rose 5%, U.S. large caps outperformed small caps, and growth outperformed value.

Persistently high shelter costs pushed November’s annualized rate of U.S. headline Consumer Price Index (CPI) inflation back up to +2.7%, while annualized core CPI inflation held steady at 3.3%. New U.S. job growth rebounded in November, while the unemployment rate ticked up to 4.2%. U.S. annualized real gross domestic product (GDP) expanded by 3.1% during the third quarter of 2024. The consensus estimates for fourth quarter real GDP growth are now ~3%, reflecting growing confidence in the health of the U.S. economy.

In December, the Federal Open Market Committee reduced the federal funds rate by another 25 basis points (bps; 100 bps equal 1.00%), down to a target range of between 4.25% and 4.50%. However, in an increasingly hawkish shift, the U.S. central bank projected only two additional 25-bp rate cuts in 2025, lowering the federal funds rate to approximately 3.90% by the end of 2025.

Figures quoted represent past performance, which is noguarantee of future results, and donotreflect taxes a shareholder maypay on an investment in a fund. Investment return and principal value of an investment will fluctuate so that an investor’s shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost. Current performance maybe lower or higher than the performance data quoted and assumes the reinvestment of dividends and capital gains. Current month-end performance is available at the fund’s website, allspringglobal.com. Institutional Class shares are sold without a front-end sales charge or contingent deferred sales charge.

The fund’s gross expense ratio is 0.51%. The fund’s net expense ratio is 0.48%. The manager has contractually committed, through November 30, 2025, to waive fees and/or reimburse expenses to the extent necessary to cap the fund’s total annual fund operating expenses after fee waivers at 0.48% for the Institutional Class. Brokerage commissions, stamp duty fees, interest, taxes, acquired fund fees and expenses (if any), and extraordinary expenses are excluded from the expense cap. Prior to or after the commitment expiration date, the cap maybe increased or the commitment to maintain the cap maybe terminated only with the approval of the Board of Trustees. Without this cap, the fund’s returns would have been lower. The expense ratio paid by an investor is the net expense ratio (the total annual fund operating expenses after fee waivers) as stated in the prospectus.

Portfolio Positioning: Quarterly Attribution and Analysis

Portfolio characteristics include: 130–220 holdings, a target sector weight +/-1% relative to the benchmark, and identification of attractively valued companies with improving earnings fundamentals relative to the benchmark.

The fund returned 4.18% for the quarter, while the benchmark S&P 500 Index returned 2.41%. The fund’s outperformance was attributed primarily to positive stock selection within IT, industrials, and health care. Negative stock selection within utilities and energy detracted from performance. The alpha model results were positive for the quarter. The momentum and quality factor groups showed strength, while value was also positive. From a risk factor perspective, the strategy’s positive exposures to beta and momentum contributed to performance. The strategy’s positive exposures to earnings yield, value, and profitability and a negative exposure to residual volatility detracted from performance. These relative risk exposures were modest. During the quarter, the strategy rotated into IT and consumer staples and away from communication services and health care stocks. Within IT, we added to hardware and software companies that should benefit as the artificial intelligence trade rotates from the earlier gains in semiconductors. We added to lower-beta consumer staples and trimmed higher-beta biotechnology companies to maintain beta 1 as higher-beta names performed strongly during the quarter. Within financials, the strategy rotated from financial services companies to insurance stocks that trade at more attractive multiples and typically benefit from lower interest rates and a less restrictive regulatory environment.

Portfolio Positioning: Trailing 12-Month Attribution Analysis

Over the past 12 months, the fund returned 29.72% while the S&P 500 Index returned 25.02%. The outperformance was attributed primarily to positive stock selection within IT, industrials, and financials. Negative stock selection within real estate and energy detracted from relative performance. Over the longer-term, broad diversification and risk controls surrounding sector weights have typically resulted in stock selection being the main driver of performance within the strategy. Regarding macroeconomic risks, the portfolio has maintained tight exposure to inflation shocks and oil as a means of weathering the volatility in commodities and geopolitical risks. Despite the Federal Reserve’s (Fed’s) reduction of short-term interest rates by 75 bps since September and expectations for two additional cuts in 2025, long-term rates have risen nearly 100 bps since the Fed’s rate cutting cycle began. Accounting for the potential risks and volatility in interest rates, the strategy has maintained neutral exposure to long and short interest rates. The portfolio was also neutral to credit spreads, which fell substantially during 2024 and now sit at levels not seen since 2005. Our experience demonstrates that fundamentally weighted strategies that invest in relatively inexpensive companies with improving fundamentals typically reward long-term investors. We continue to build portfolios that adhere to this philosophy while recognizing that the current pervasive macroeconomic risks require an elevated emphasis on risk controls, particularly as they relate to industry exposures, secular growth, oil prices, inflation expectations, and credit risk. We still prefer an emphasis on secular value (i.e., quality and value) over risk and beta, as we continue through the later phases of the economic cycle.

Portfolio Positioning: Outlook

As we enter 2025, it appears the U.S. has achieved a soft economic landing. Continued disinflation, lower interest rates, and a strong labor market have eliminated most near-term U.S. recession risks. In September 2024, the U.S. Federal Reserve (Fed) cut rates for the first time since the 2020 onset of the COVID-19 pandemic, concluding the Fed’s fastest tightening cycle in more than four decades. However, the persistence of inflation, strong economic growth, and low unemployment should force the U.S. central bank to take a more measured approach in 2025. Despite the looming threat of a tariff war by the incoming Trump administration, U.S. GDP growth will likely continue outperforming other developed markets, as monetary policy may shift rapidly from a significant headwind into an economic tailwind.

Since mid-July, equity market returns have broadened out from the “Magnificent Seven” (Alphabet, Amazon, Apple, Meta, Microsoft, NVIDIA, and Tesla), as the “other 493 stocks” have performed relatively well. After the concentrated mega-cap rally of 2023 and throughout most of 2024, equity markets in 2025 are likely to be defined by slowing global economic growth, single-digit earnings growth, and a broadening of returns. We think that volatility is likely amid a looming tariff war and ongoing geopolitical tensions surrounding Ukraine, the Middle East, and Taiwan. Given this backdrop, we expect 2025 to provide opportunities for disciplined stock selection to add value as it did during 2024. We currently anticipate volatile and range-bound markets and are mindful to neutralize our exposures to macroeconomic and geopolitical risks where possible. While we are generally cautious, there are potential positive catalysts from decelerating inflation, lower rates, a resilient U.S. job market, improving corporate earnings, and a broadening of market returns.

TOP CONTRIBUTORS AND DETRACTORS TO QUARTER-END FUND PERFORMANCE

data.title: Agree Realty: When The Perfect Blend Of Consistency, Reliability, And Quality Meet
data.description:
Introduction

When it comes to Agree Realty (NYSE:ADC), the REIT has all the makings of an obvious long-term hold. Although REITs have been somewhat disappointing investments lately due to macro environmental headwinds, long-term income investors should be looking to the sector for value.

What also makes Agree Realty a great investment is management's Quality over Quantity embodiment. ADC takes pride in their strong portfolio consisting of the biggest and best retailers in the country.

I also enjoy the fact that management never waivers nor deviates from their core investment strategy of acquiring high-quality properties and minimizing the possibility of delivering mediocre returns.

Moreover, their limited susceptibility to economic downturns as a result of their high-exposure to recession-resistant tenants and strong growth runway, gives the stock significant upside over the long term in my opinion.

In this article, I discuss Agree Realty's latest earnings, financials, and why this high quality REIT is a must-own in an income-focused portfolio despite double-digit price appreciation in the past year.

Previous Buy Thesis

I last covered Agree Realty this past October in an article titled: Agree Realty: Quality Always Rises To The Top. At a then forward P/AFFO multiple of 17.86x, ADC offered significant upside potential as they traded well-below their 5-year average of 20.14x.

As stated in the article, I suspected Agree Realty could reach its 5-year average or above over the next 12 to 24 months. This gave the REIT double-digit upside to a price target of $86.

Since then, inflation worries have kept the stock's share price relatively flat, down 3.66% in comparison to the S&P who is in the green by an almost identical amount, 3.68%.

ADC's Q3 earnings saw them raise full-year guidance again, this time to a range of $4.12 - $4.14. And despite core FFO declining from the previous quarter, this along with their revenue grew solidly year-over-year. The REIT's balance sheet and strong acquisitions also allowed them to raise their acquisition and disposition guidance for the year.

Q4 & Full-Year Performance

ADC's fourth quarter and full-year performance again showed why they remain one of the best net lease REITs to own. During earnings, the REIT beat on both their top and bottom lines, a further testament to their high-quality considering the challenging economic backdrop.

AFFO of $1.04 managed to beat analysts' estimates by $0.02 while revenue of $160.73 million beat by a solid $1.58 million. This brought their full-year AFFO right at the top of management's guidance range of $4.14. This resulted in a 4.8% increase year-over-year from 2023's $3.95.

For comparison purposes, peer NNN REIT (NNN) saw a 2.8% increase in AFFO year-over-year to $3.35 a share.

During the quarter, AFFO increased 4% from $1.00 in the prior year's quarter. In Q4, their strong growth continued as the REIT invested $371 million in 127 net lease properties. This was higher than the previous quarter that saw management invest $237 million across 90 properties.

And brought ADC's total property count to 2,370 properties, up from 2,271. Year-over-year, their property count grew from 2,135 properties. Considering their strong growth from the previous year, ADC's growth runway remains long, with less than 2,500 properties.

And this doesn't even include the fact they haven't tapped into the international space yet, which could come much further in the future.

For the year, they invested $951 million in 282 properties and commenced development on an additional 25 properties worth $115 million. For the quarter, acquisitions amounted to roughly $342 million in 98 properties, acquired at a weighted-average cap rate of 7.3%, slightly higher than last year that saw a weighted-average cap rate of 7.2%.

Acquisitions for the full-year totaled $866 million, exceeding the $850 million set in Q3.

Dispositions in the quarter totaled 8 properties and 26 properties for the full-year at a WACR of 6.7%, resulting in 80 basis points investment spreads and generating robust accretion for shareholders. For the year, dispositions netted the REIT $98.4 million in proceeds.

2025 Guidance & Long-Term Position

Another metric that makes Agree Realty the top net lease REIT that is likely to drive strong returns for shareholders is the company's fortress of a balance sheet. Over the last several months, management took advantage of the sector rally in July by selling shares, further strengthening their liquidity, positioning the REIT well for 2025 and beyond.

Their net debt to EBITDA was a strong 4.9x and 3.3x on a pro forma basis. This is in comparison to NNN's 5.5x. ADC's minimal debt maturities and robust liquidity that stood at $2 billion further solidifies why they're likely poised for further upside.

This allows the REIT the flexibility to continue to see solid growth, both externally and internally. They also extended their revolver to $1.25 billion and maturity to August of 2029. And as Agree Realty continues to acquire properties and see tailwinds from lower interest rates, the REIT is likely to see strong price appreciation over the long term.

Management issued 2025 AFFO guidance of $4.26 - $4.30, representing a solid 3.38% at midpoint. But like 2024, I expect the goal post to move to the right for ADC. Additionally, acquisitions are expected to be between $1.1 billion to $1.3 billion for the year and disposition volume in a range of $10 million to $50 million.

Well-Covered Monthly Dividend

Shareholders also get paid a well-covered monthly dividend while they wait for potential upside, which I see plenty over the next 12 to 24 months. ADC ended the year with an AFFO payout ratio of 73.3%. This was in comparison to NNN's AFFO payout ratio of 68.2%.

However, both were very conservative for REITs in general. And this gives Agree Realty ample room for consistent and steady dividend increases, which I suspect will continue for the foreseeable future. The REIT was also approaching $100 million in retained income after dividends, giving them additional capital for potential M&A activity.

Ground Lease Portfolio

Agree's hidden gem is their ground lease portfolio, which has even higher exposure to investment-grade tenants than their net lease property portfolio. During the quarter, the REIT acquired an additional 6 properties to bring their total to 229 across 36 states.

And I expect management to focus on growing this for the foreseeable future. At the end of the year, the portfolio was 100% occupied with 88.2% exposure to investment-rated tenants.

Upside Potential/Risks

Although Agree Realty trades at a higher premium in comparison to many of its peers, I think the REIT is poised to see strong upside as the macro environmental picture becomes clearer.

Additionally, their forward multiple of 16.58x is well-below their 5-year average and not much higher than the sector median's 16.10x. If interest rates decline this year and going forward, I expect ADC could trade somewhere in between their regular and high price target of $89.

However, the hotter than expected recent CPI report could result in higher for longer interest rates. And if they do remain steady for 2025, this will result in limited upside for Agree Realty as volatility will likely pick up in treasuries.

Some economists are even calling for a rate hike as early as this summer. Although I think that scenario is unlikely, surging inflation in the future could force the FED's hand. If so, this would most certainly result in downside for Agree Realty as well as the entire sector.

Bottom Line

With a forward P/E of 16.58x using 2025's guidance midpoint, Agree Realty trades significantly below their 5-year average. Quality usually doesn't come cheap, and the recent pullback from their 52-week high in recent months should be viewed as a long-term buying opportunity.

With a perfect blend of quality, reliability, and consistency, Agree Realty is the perfect stock to put inside of an income-focused portfolio that could help further grow your wealth. As a result of this and their strong runway for growth, I continue to rate Agree Realty a buy.

data.title: Hewlett Packard Enterprise: The Cheapest Great AI Server Provider You Can Buy
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Hewlett Packard Enterprise Company (NYSE:HPE) is one of the world's leading IT enterprise solution providers. The company is known for its servers, storage, networking, and software solutions that enterprises need to enable their IT infrastructure. With the rise of AI, the company is strategically refocusing on this industry as it can provide the growth that can propel the company out of stagnation.

Based on what I've seen in HPE's AI Day presentation, I believe that they have a sound strategy to capitalize on the growing AI industry by accelerating enterprise AI adoption. Additionally, HPE's integrated solutions, from financing, storage, computing, and networking to software, will play a crucial role in its strategy to accelerate AI adoption among enterprises. With HPE being the cheapest great AI server provider stock you can buy right now, I believe this presents a wonderful opportunity for investors, especially value-seeking ones, who are hesitant to invest in AI right now due to how expensive most of the stocks participating in this area are. Thus, I will call a 'Buy' rating for this HPE.

Accelerating the Enterprise AI Adoption

In HPE's AI Day presentation, the management identified key growth areas with which they believe the company has an opportunity.

In their presentation, the AI market is segmented into four categories: Hyperscalers, Service Providers, Sovereigns, and Enterprises. While the whole market is projected to grow at a CAGR of 25%, HPE's real opportunity will be in the sovereign and enterprises. However, there are opportunities among model builders and AI service providers.

Hyperscalers & Service Providers

Hyperscalers are big cloud players like Google (GOOG) (GOOGL), Amazon (AMZN), and Microsoft (MSFT) that build their own AI infrastructure to train AI models. HPE has limited opportunity on traditional hyperscalers, as they typically source their AI servers through Original Direct Manufacturers or ODMs. The real opportunities are among the smaller model builders, whose AI models are trained for specialized needs such as healthcare, law, cybersecurity, and more. These customers are usually small start-ups with limited access to capital that can benefit from HPE's pay-as-you-go model. This circumvents the huge CAPEX usually associated with AI infrastructure buildouts.

HPE also sees opportunities among AI service providers. Service providers enable customers to deliver value from their AI investments and can add HPE's infrastructure expertise to a turnkey solution that their customers usually seek. Service providers play a crucial role in HPE's enterprise AI strategy as they possess differentiated value-adding activities that enterprises, businesses, and model-builders can use to realize value from AI.

Additionally, HPE's integrated solution, which includes storage, networking, and servers, will be well received by these customers as it simplifies the infrastructure buildout process. They can focus less on the infrastructure and more on running their businesses.

Sovereign & Enterprise

HPE's biggest AI opportunity will be in this segment as it plays to its years of experience in enabling hybrid cloud implementation for enterprise and sovereign customers. They also have existing customer relations in this segment that they can leverage to accelerate their enterprise AI strategy. With the help of AI service partners, HPE can provide turnkey AI solutions for enterprises seeking to integrate AI into their operations easily. Due to a lack of AI expertise, partly due to the shortage of AI talents, enterprises are more inclined to pay a premium for these complete AI solutions, thus providing an accretive margin opportunity for HPE. The HPE's traditional pay-as-you-go model will be a significant value proposition for enterprises as they prefer to avoid huge upfront CAPEX investments, especially for a technology whose value creation capability for a business is still unproven or uncertain.

Additionally, HPE's scalable solution will allow enterprises to run their proofs-of-concept as cheaply as possible and easily scale once pushed into production. This can be a significant value proposition as there is still a lot of uncertainty regarding AI use cases among enterprises. The companies that can clear the fog in AI towards a value-creating outcome through their comprehensive solution will have a significant advantage over others that only provide part of the solution. I believe that HPE will be one of those companies.

Rising Deferred Revenue

One of the best measures for companies like HPE, due to its service products and long-term pay-as-you-go arrangement with its Greenlake solution, is its deferred revenue.

Since 2018, HPE's deferred revenue has been stagnant, which indicates a lack of interest in HPE's products and services. This can be attributed to the industry's maturity and intense competition from Dell (DELL) and Lenovo (OTCPK:LNVGY). However, the rise of AI in 2023 altered this trend and provided a catalyst for the entire industry to grow. Since 2023, HPE has witnessed significant growth in its deferred revenue, which indicates a growing demand for its products and services; it can be an early indicator of the company's future growth. Additionally, rising deferred revenue growth indicates whether HPE's strategy is gaining traction or not. Thus, it is one important metric we should monitor moving forward.

HPE's Acquisition Of Juniper Will Strengthen Their Enterprise AI Portfolio

Last year, HPE announced its intention to acquire Juniper Networks (JNPR). HPE management believes that Juniper's product portfolio will strengthen its enterprise networking portfolio. HPE can gain access to Juniper's high-performance networking solution to strengthen its AI infrastructure solution for enterprise customers. There are also synergistic benefits as both companies gain access to each other's customers, effectively increasing the total addressable market of HPE and Juniper. I also believe that the integration effort, which has been the bane of many corporate M&A failures in the past, will be significantly easier because of the similarity of both businesses and the complementary nature of both businesses towards each other. And based on the management's rationale for the acquisition, this seems to be the case.

Before I pass it over to Rami, which is here with me today, I would like to quickly stress that is also make a strategic sense as it aligns two complementary businesses with similar cultures. It is a natural fit. HPE and Juniper have a similar commitment to innovation and relentless focus on our customers. And Juniper has got an incredible strong team. And we're looking forward to bringing our two companies together.

Interpreting what management is saying here, it seems that the intention for the acquisition is more for Juniper's assets: its customer relationships, product portfolio, assets, and distribution channel. Integration of this type is far easier because of the similarity of the value and process that converts input into output. Acquisitions are typically a disaster if a company is integrating another company whose culture, value, and processes are totally misaligned with the acquirer. For example, integrating a discount store into an upscale store will be a disaster due to the significant difference in the processes, values, and cultures that guide both businesses to excel in their respective markets. The logical choice for this kind of acquisition is to give the acquired company autonomy. In HPE's case, I believe plug-in integration will be logical. You integrate the ones you need and remove the redundant ones.

I believe HPE's acquisition of Juniper will deliver value for the shareholders in the long term due to the clear synergistic benefit and ease of integration. The acquisition is also manageable from a financial standpoint.

On a pro forma basis, the combined EBITDA of both companies will generate around $5.67B, around 3.5x of HPE's current debt, which is manageable. It is still close to Dell's current Debt/EBITDA leverage ratio of around 2.8x. Please note that I divide the $450M cost synergy that the management believes will be achievable within 36 months by three($450M / 3 years = $150M cost savings per year).

This deal still faces regulatory hurdles, but I believe that the DOJ's argument is weak due to how competitive the enterprise WLAN market is. This market will still have multiple competitors even if HPE and Juniper merge. I'm inclined that this deal will go through.

Valuation

The good thing about investing in AI is that regardless of a firm's strategy, everyone can succeed in this growing industry as long as the company imitates what others are doing successfully. This is until the industry matures, cutthroat competition takes over, and everyone's profitability goes down the drain. But AI is still a young industry, so we don't need to worry about it for a while. Of course, strategy matters, and you can grow quicker than the industry average if you have the right strategy. Considering the AI tailwinds, I believe that the best method to valuate HPE is its earnings yield and how it compares to its competitors. I will calculate earnings yield by simply dividing HPE's current EBIT by its current enterprise value.

With an earnings yield of 7.34%, you get exposure to the high-growth potential of the AI industry. Compared to an alternative no-growth 4.5% risk-free 10-year US bond yield, I believe buying HPE is a much better deal and worth the risk. Therefore, I will call a 'Buy' for this stock. Why just a 'Buy' rating? Because, as a value investor, I prefer a lower price tag.

Risk

Enterprise Adoption

The investment thesis laid out in this article heavily relies on the adoption of AI among enterprises. Enterprises are still struggling to integrate AI into their business operations, and there is still uncertainty about the return on investments with AI. The anticipated adoption of AI in enterprises presented in this article may not happen or take longer than expected, which could affect HPE's valuation. Despite the clear and significant hurdle of how enterprises will integrate AI into their operations, this also presents an opportunity for HPE. Enterprises overcoming this hurdle with HPE will create customer relationships that can be beneficial down the road due to reciprocation.

Competition

HPE also faces intense competition in all segments presented in their AI Day. Original direct manufacturers are the typical go-to solution for hyperscalers due to their specialized AI server designs. In the sovereign and enterprises, HPE faced intense competition from Dell and Lenovo, which all have similar business models. They offer similar products, a pay-as-you-go model, and turnkey solutions, and they also have relationships with the same AI service providers. Hyperscalers also compete with HPE's hybrid cloud business model. Enterprises may choose to run their AI workload in the cloud instead of HPE's private cloud. However, the good thing is that AI is a high-growth industry. It presents an opportunity for all its participants to achieve respectable growth without aggravating competition, resulting in cutthroat competition that can impede everyone's growth and profitability.
